project_acceptance.observable: There is a billing/proration.py whose prorate_cents(amount_cents, days_used, days_in_period) hands back an int number of cents, computed without any float or Decimal.
The worked examples hold: prorate_cents(999, 10, 30) == 333, prorate_cents(125, 1, 2) == 63, prorate_cents(101, 1, 2) == 51 and prorate_cents(1999, 7, 31) == 451.

days_used never escapes the period: prorate_cents(1000, 45, 30) == 1000, prorate_cents(1000, -3, 30) == 0, prorate_cents(500, 0, 30) == 0 — a prorated charge is never more than the full amount and never negative.
days_in_period <= 0 raises ValueError; a non-int amount_cents raises TypeError.
Invoice.add_prorated_line(description, amount_cents, days_used, days_in_period) puts a single ordinary (description, int amount_cents) line on the invoice, and total_cents() keeps returning an int.
billing/money.py is untouched and `python3 -m unittest discover -s tests` passes.

tasks.objective: Create billing/proration.py holding prorate_cents(amount_cents, days_used, days_in_period) -> int, the share of amount_cents owed for days_used of days_in_period days. It works in integer cents throughout — a float or a Decimal must never appear in the maths or the result. Rounding goes to the nearest cent with a tie going away from zero (half up), which is what billing.money.round_half_up already does, so call it instead of writing new rounding and never drop the fractional cent by truncating. Clamp days_used into [0, days_in_period] before prorating, so the charge can neither exceed amount_cents nor fall below zero. Raise ValueError when days_in_period <= 0, and TypeError when amount_cents is not an int.

context_refs.claim: round_half_up(numerator, denominator) already divides two ints rounding to the nearest whole unit with ties away from zero, using integer arithmetic only, and raises TypeError on non-int input and ValueError on a non-positive denominator.
Every amount in Ledgerloom is an integer number of cents; billing is not allowed to carry money as a float.
Plan prices are integer cents per month (starter 999, team 1999, studio 4900), which is what gets prorated.

must_preserve: billing/money.py: round_half_up must not be modified — the rest of billing depends on its exact behaviour
money stays int cents end to end; no float or Decimal enters billing

acceptance.observable: A half-cent tie rounds up, away from zero: prorate_cents(125, 1, 2) == 63 (not 62), prorate_cents(101, 1, 2) == 51 (not 50).
Out-of-range days are clamped into the period: prorate_cents(1000, 45, 30) == 1000, prorate_cents(1000, -3, 30) == 0, prorate_cents(500, 0, 30) == 0.
prorate_cents(999, 10, 0) raises ValueError; prorate_cents(9.99, 10, 30) raises TypeError.

tasks.handoff: prorate_cents exists: int cents, half-up rounding, days clamped to the period.

tasks.objective: Give Invoice a new method add_prorated_line(description, amount_cents, days_used, days_in_period) in billing/invoice.py: it runs the amount through prorate_cents and appends the prorated int cents as a single normal line. The line shape stays what it is today — description plus int amount_cents — and total_cents() goes on returning an int.

context_refs.claim: add_line appends a (description, amount_cents) pair and rejects non-int amounts; total_cents sums the line amounts.
Invoice.lines is a list of (description, amount_cents) pairs where amount_cents is always an int.

must_preserve: the (description, amount_cents) line shape, with amount_cents an int
Invoice.add_line, Invoice.total_cents and Invoice.render behave exactly as they do now

acceptance.observable: invoice.add_prorated_line("Team plan", 1999, 7, 31) leaves the invoice with the single line ('Team plan', 451) and total_cents() == 451.
An invoice mixing add_line and add_prorated_line still totals to an int.
lines still holds (description, amount_cents) pairs; no new shape is introduced.

tasks.handoff: A prorated charge can go on an invoice as an ordinary int-cent line.

tasks.objective: Add tests under tests/ for all of it: the worked examples, ties rounding up, the clamped days, the ValueError / TypeError cases, and the prorated invoice line. `python3 -m unittest discover -s tests` must pass.

context_refs.claim: The existing money tests show the house style for pinning rounding behaviour, including the tie cases and the int return type.
The existing invoice tests assert on the total and on the (description, amount_cents) line shape.

must_preserve: the existing money and invoice tests keep passing unchanged

acceptance.observable: `python3 -m unittest discover -s tests` passes with no failures.
The tie cases are pinned: prorate_cents(125, 1, 2) == 63 and prorate_cents(101, 1, 2) == 51.
The clamped cases are pinned: prorate_cents(1000, 45, 30) == 1000 and prorate_cents(1000, -3, 30) == 0.
A test shows add_prorated_line yields an int-cent line and an int total.

tasks.handoff: The suite pins the proration rules and the prorated invoice line.

assumptions.statement: Periods are whole days, so days_used and days_in_period are ints; nothing shorter than a day is prorated.
Proration calls the existing round_half_up instead of introducing a second rounding rule, so all of billing rounds identically.
